prompt: an onchain agents hackathon with five sponsors and a $5k pool

bounties.title: Best onchain UX for permits
bounties.description: Build the smoothest two-tap permit flow. Surface gas, surface failure, no hidden state. Web only, no extension.
qualification: No external network calls beyond an RPC stub

bounties.title: Make the mesh visible
bounties.description: Visualise an AXL mesh in real time. Show peers, show traffic, show topology. Make a non-engineer understand.
qualification: Updates within 200ms of a new event

bounties.title: Build something playful
bounties.description: Take a serious primitive and make it joyful. The judges will play it. Make it want to be played.

bounties.title: Show the judging
bounties.description: Reveal how three independent judges saw the same project. Make the disagreement legible, not a bar chart of total scores.

bounties.title: Best open-source contribution
bounties.description: Submit your project under MIT or Apache. Bonus for a working pull request against an upstream Gensyn repo.

builders.persona_excerpt: Loves writing tiny scenes that hit 60fps on a phone. Believes the mesh has a shape and the shape is a cathedral.
Reads docs end to end before writing a line. Force-directed graphs are a hammer; many things look like nails.
Has signed more permits than most people have signed birthday cards. Treats UX as a security feature.
Has strong opinions about colour, kerning, and why most progress bars lie.
Started writing pong clones at 12, never stopped. Believes any concept can be a two-paddle game.
Adds a sound effect to anything that moves. Sound is feedback, feedback is feel, feel is good.

projects.title: Tree of Skill
projects.tagline: A D3 force graph of every builder, coloured by skill cluster.
projects.description: A live force-directed graph that pulses on every team-formed event and recolours when a builder changes track. Hovering a node reveals the builder's skills.

projects.title: Permit Cathedral
projects.tagline: A three.js scene where each peer is a glowing column on the floor.
projects.description: Walk the cathedral. Each column lights up when its peer broadcasts. The room hums in proportion to mesh traffic. WASD to move. Click a column to read the latest envelope it sent.

projects.title: Permit Pong
projects.tagline: Pong, but each rally is a signed permit.
projects.description: Two-paddle pong where every paddle hit triggers an EIP-2612 permit. Watch the ball, watch the signatures fly across the bottom strip. Score to ten.

rubric.name: Functionality
rubric.description: Does it work end to end on a fresh load.

rubric.name: Originality
rubric.description: Did anyone else think to do this.

rubric.name: Use of AXL
rubric.description: Does the project actually exercise the mesh.

verdicts.feedback: The graph reflowed cleanly on every team-formed event. Colour clusters were legible. Hover popovers landed on the wrong node twice during my test, lost a point on functionality.
verdicts.interactions_summary: click on a node, hover over a node, drag a node

verdicts.feedback: Tidy code, separation of concerns is good. The simulation loop runs even when offscreen, mild perf concern.
Best mesh-aware project of the three. The graph reads peer ids, the layout reflects topology. Clean.
Beautiful. The hum is a clever audio cue. Movement clipping in one corner cost a point.
verdicts.interactions_summary: WASD to move, click on a column

verdicts.feedback: Polish is high. Audio context not gated behind user gesture, throws a warning in console.
Each column maps to a real peer id. Decent. The bounty asked for permit UX though, this leans on the visualisation lane.
Pong with permits is a good gag, and the gag holds up. The permit overlay covers the ball at high score deltas.
verdicts.interactions_summary: ArrowUp, ArrowDown, hold space to charge

verdicts.feedback: Clean game loop. Sound effects belong on a toggle, they auto-played on load.
AXL angle is light. Permits are signed locally; nothing crosses the mesh. Loses points there.
